texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separations of binary CO2/CH4 and C2H4/C2H6 mixtures are required for natural gas puri fi cation and petroleum re fi ning processes. Both amine absorption for CO2 removal and cryogenic distillation for ole fi n/paraf fi n separation are energyand costintensive processes. Membrane technology can play a key role in making these separations economically feasible. Carbon dioxideselective polymeric membranes were installed in the 1980s for natural gas puri fi cation [1]. However, high CO2 pressures can plasticize polymeric membranes and decrease their separation ability [2]. Zeolite membranes such as zeolite T [3], DDR [4], MFI [5], SAPO-34 [6 -8] and AlPO-18 [9] and meta-organic framework (MOF) membranes [10] can separate CO2 from CH4. The SAPO-34 membranes grown on asymmetric alumina tubes had good separation performances for CO2/CH4 mixtures at high pressures [8]. Also, conventional polymeric membranes cannot achieve the permeability and selectivity combinations for C2H4/C2H6 separations, and the membrane stability for most polymeric materials under actual aggressive hydrocarbon feeds requires consideration. Inorganic membranes have potentials for the application of ole fi n/ paraf fi n separations. Pan and Lai [11] and Kwon and Jeong [12] reported that ZIF-8 membranes showed relatively high C2H4/C3H8

texts.label: text
texts.orig: High-quality SSZ-13 membranes were synthesized using the combinative structure-directing agents of N , N , N , trimethtyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide and tetraethylammonium hydroxide by one hydrothermal treatment step. The two organics took a ' cooperative structural-directing ' role on SSZ-13 crystallization. The best membrane had a CO2 permeance of 2.0 -10 � 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) with a CO2/CH4 selectivity of 300 and a C2H4 permeance of 2.9 -10 � 9 mol/(m 2 s Pa) with a C2H4/C2H6 selectivity of 11, at 303 K and 0.2 MPa feed pressure for these equimolar binary mixtures, respectively. SSZ-13 membranes also displayed high performances for CO2/CH4 and C2H4/C2H6 separations at higher feed pressures of 0.8 -1 MPa. These SSZ-13 membranes were hydrothermally stable at steam for at least 4 days at 378 K and acid-resistance at pH ¼ 1.

texts.orig: High-silica CHA zeolite (SSZ-13) has a 3-dimensional structure with 8-membered rings and intersecting channels with a ring diameter of 0.37 nm -0.42 nm [17]. SSZ-13 and all-silica CHA zeolites displayed highly CO2-selective adsorption abilities over N2 and CH4; the CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 adsorption selectivities were up to 70 [18] and 4.1 [19] at room temperature, respectively. The pore size of this kind of zeolite, together with its selective adsorption properties, makes it promising for use in membranes to separate light hydrocarbon mixtures. Previously, SSZ-13 crystals [20,21] and membranes [22,23] were synthesized using N , N , N , trimethtyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide (TMAdaOH) as the structure-directing agent (SDA). A SSZ-13 membrane with a CO2/CH4 separation selectivity of 13 was fi rstly reported by Kalipcilar et al. [23]; the membrane was prepared on the inside of porous stainless steel supports by two hydrothermal synthesis steps.

texts.orig: In the current study, SSZ-13 membranes with extremely low defect concentrations were prepared in one synthesis step on the outer surface of a symmetric mullite tube using TMAdaOH and tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH) as SDAs. The synergistic structure-directing effect of the two SDAs played a key role in forming dense SSZ-13 layers. The membranes showed high performance for CO2/CH4 and C2H4/C2H6 separations .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Synthesis procedure for SSZ-13 seeds
texts.text: 2.1. Synthesis procedure for SSZ-13 seeds

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SSZ-13 seed crystals with an average size of 500 nm were synthesized using a procedure similar to that described by Zones [20]. The molar composition of the gel was 1.0 SiO2: 0.10 Na2O: 0.025 Al2O3: 0.40 TMAdaOH: 44 H2O. The SDA TMAdaOH was prepared according to the literature [21]. The seeds gel was prepared by mixing 0.88 g of NaOH (96%, Sinopharm), 0.41 g of Al(OH)3 powder (99%, Wako) and 41.0 g of DI water and stirring the mixture at room temperature for 1 h to form a homogenous aluminate solution. Then 35.8 g of TMAdaOH (25 wt% in water) was slowly added to the solution for 15 min, and the solution was stirred for 1 h without heating. Finally, 15.9 g of colloidal silica (Ludox TM-40, Sigma-Aldrich) was added to the solution. The mixtures were aged overnight at room temperature with stirring. The gel was transferred to a Te fl on-lined autoclave and placed into an oven at 433 K for 96 h. After synthesis, the SSZ-13 crystals were recovered by centrifugation, washed three times in DI water and dried overnight at 373 K. The template was removed from the crystals in air at 823 K for 10 h with heating and cooling rates of 10 K/min.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Synthesis procedure for SSZ-13 membranes
texts.text: 2.2. Synthesis procedure for SSZ-13 membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membranes were hydrothermally synthesized onto the outside surface of porous mullite tubes. The mullite tubes (12 mm OD, 8 mm ID and 1.3 m maverage pore size) from Nikkato Company were cut into 10 cm long pieces and polished using 800# sandpaper and washed several times with boiling DI water for 30 min and dried overnight at 373 K. Prior to membrane preparation, the outer surface of the support was coated by rubbing it with an aqueous slurry containing 10 wt% seed crystals for approximately 1 min. The best SSZ-13 membranes were synthesized using dual SDAs of TMAdaOH and TEAOH and membrane gel was prepared by mixed NaOH (96%, Sinopharm), Al (OH)3 (99%, Wako), TMAdaOH (25 wt% in water), TEAOH (35 wt% in water, Sigma-Aldrich) colloidal silica (Ludox TM-40, Sigma-Aldrich) and DI water. These gels were aged at room temperature for 2 days while stirring to obtain an uniform gel. The resulted membrane gel had a molar ratio of 1.0 SiO2: 0.1 Na2O: 0.025 Al2O3: 0.05 TMAdaOH: 0.05 TEAOH: 80 H2O. The seeded supports were then placed in an autoclave, which was then fi lled with membrane gel to about 2 cm above the top of the supports. The hydrothermal treatment was carried out in a conventional oven at 443 K for 8 -48 h. The resulting membranes were washed in running tap water for 15 min, soaked in deionized water overnight and dried at 373 K for 12 h. The membranes were calcined in stagnant air at 753 K for 6 h with heating and cooling rates of 0.5 K/min, and then stored at 393 K. To investigate the effect of SDA on membrane growth, a single SDA [TMAdaOH, TEAOH or tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH)] (SDA/SiO2 ¼ 0.05), dual SDAs (TMAdaOH/TMAOH/SiO2 ¼ 0.05/0.05/1) and ternary SDAs (TMAdaOH/TEAOH/TMAOH/SiO2 ¼ 0.05/0.05/0.05/1) were used.

The crystal phases of the crystals and membranes were identi fi ed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Ultima IV) using Cu K α radiation with 2 θ from 5 1 to 45 1 at a step size of 0.05 1 . Note that XRD measurements can be made on the tubular membrane without breaking it. The 13 C magic-angle spinning nuclear magnetic resonance (MAS NMR) spectra of the zeolite powders were recorded on a Bruker Advance III 400 WB spectrometer with a spinning rate of 4 kHz at a 13 C frequency of 100.63 MHz, using a zirconia rotor of 4 mm diameter. The spectra were accumulated with 4 ms pulses, 2 s recycle delay and 1024 scans for 13 C MAS NMR. The 13 C chemical shifts are reported relative to tetramethylsilane (TMS). Prior to the NMR test, the as-synthesized crystals were washed by dimethylformamide for three times and then by water for three times with each 30 min sonication, and dried under vacuum at 120 1 C overnight to remove the attached organics.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Single-gas permeation and mixed-gas separations were measured using the Wicke -Kallenbach method with helium as a sweep gas at 303 K. The feed pressure could be varied from 0.1 to 1.0 MPa. The membranes were mounted in a stainless steel module, and sealed at each end with three silicone O -rings and two stainless steel rings in turn. The feed gas went through the gap (6 mm) between the membrane and the module. Mass fl ow controllers were used to mix pure gases in equimolar ratio, and the compositions of the feed, retentate and permeate streams were measured by a GC (Shimadzu GC-14C) with a thermal conductivity detector. The fl ow rates for each composition in the binary mixtures and the helium sweep gas were 150 and 100 standard mL/min (SMLPM), respectively. All the gases are highpurity grade ( Z 99.999%) and were puri fi ed with activated carbon and alumina to remove the moisture and higher hydrocarbons. The ideal and mixed-gas selectivities are the ratio of the single-gas permeances and the ratios of mixture permeances, respectively. Each data point was obtained after a 2 h stabilization time, and changes in permeances of each component was lower than 3% in 1 h. It took approximately 6 h for the permeance tests of single and mixed lighter gases (H2, N2, CO2 and CH4) and approximately 12 h for these of higher hydrocarbons (C2H4, C2H6 and C3H8), respectively. For the permeation test of single propane through SSZ-13 membrane M2, the propane permeance was not recorded since the contents of propane in the permeate side is lower than the GC detection limit (  0.5 ppm). The effective membrane area was about 19 cm 2 . Thus, single propane permeance through SSZ-13 membrane M2 was estimated to be o 1.3 -10 -12 mol/(m 2 s Pa).

texts.orig: 3.1. Membrane preparation and morphology
texts.text: 3.1. Membrane preparation and morphology

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of calcined SSZ-13 seeds, seeded support and SSZ-13 membranes prepared with different synthesis times. These membranes were synthesized using the binary SDAs of TMAdaOH and TEAOH. The XRD peaks of SSZ-13 seeds and membranes correspond to CHA structure, indicating the seeds and the supported zeolite layers are pure CHA phase. The typical peaks of SSZ-13 crystals increased with synthesis time, suggesting the crystallinity and membrane thickness increased with synthesis time. The membranes displayed a random orientation compared with the powder XRD pattern [20]. The Si/Al ratios of the seeds and membrane surface crystals were 16 and 10, respectively, according to EDX analysis; these values are similar to that of reported SSZ-13 (Si/Al ¼ 14) [20]. The higher Al content in the membrane crystals may be resulted from alumina leaching of the mullite support (Si/Al ¼ 0.3) and the changes in gel chemistry.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The morphologies of mullite support, SSZ-13 seed layers and membranes prepared with different synthesis times are shown in Fig. 2. The mullite support had the symmetric pore structure and the surface pores were around 1 -3 m m (Fig. 2a and b). The symmetric mullite support is generally cheaper than alumina supports since its sintering temperature is lower. Moreover, the usage of mullite support is considered to reduce the leaching of aluminum from the supports because the alumina content in mullite support is lower than the alumina support. Seed crystals that had spherical or near-cubic morphologies with sizes in the 300 -600 nm range were loosely packed on the surface of the mullite support as multilayers, as shown in Fig. 2c and d. The typical seed layer thickness was  2 m m. After 8 h synthesis, a lot of crystals whose size was 1/10 of the seeds size were observed on the surface of the support and inside support, as shown in Fig. 2e and f. These small crystals had the Si/Al ratio of 10 -12 by the localarea EDX analysis and were considered to be SSZ-13 crystals formed from the membrane gel. The intergrowth behavior and membrane thickness increased as synthesis time increased from 8 to 48 h. The top-view SEM image of the membrane prepared in Fig. 2i shows dense zeolite layers composed with  1 -3 m m polycrystalline gains. The membrane thickness was  10 m m, as shown in Fig. 2j. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDXS) revealed that there were sharp transitions for Si and Al content from the silica-rich SSZ-13 layer to alumina-rich support layer in Figure S1, which further identi fi ed that the membrane thickness was about 10. Compared with the secondary growth of SSZ-13 membrane, in-situ synthesis of SSZ-13 membrane did not yield selective SSZ-13 membrane under current synthesis conditions. The pin-pore defects could be observed on the membrane surface for the typical membrane prepared for 48 h, as shown in Figure S2. It suggested that the pre-coated seeds supplied effective nuclei for the formation of an intergrowth SSZ-13 membrane.
texts.text: The morphologies of mullite support, SSZ-13 seed layers and membranes prepared with different synthesis times are shown in Fig. 2. The mullite support had the symmetric pore structure and the surface pores were around 1 -3 m m (Fig. 2a and b). The symmetric mullite support is generally cheaper than alumina supports since its sintering temperature is lower. Moreover, the usage of mullite support is considered to reduce the leaching of aluminum from the supports because the alumina content in mullite support is lower than the alumina support. Seed crystals that had spherical or near-cubic morphologies with sizes in the 300 -600 nm range were loosely packed on the surface of the mullite support as multilayers, as shown in Fig. 2c and d. The typical seed layer thickness was  2 m m. After 8 h synthesis, a lot of crystals whose size was 1/10 of the seeds size were observed on the surface of the support and inside support, as shown in Fig. 2e and f. These small crystals had the Si/Al ratio of 10 -12 by the localarea EDX analysis and were considered to be SSZ-13 crystals formed from the membrane gel. The intergrowth behavior and membrane thickness increased as synthesis time increased from 8 to 48 h. The top-view SEM image of the membrane prepared in Fig. 2i shows dense zeolite layers composed with  1 -3 m m polycrystalline gains. The membrane thickness was  10 m m, as shown in Fig. 2j. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDXS) revealed that there were sharp transitions for Si and Al content from the silica-rich SSZ-13 layer to alumina-rich support layer in Figure S1, which further identi fi ed that the membrane thickness was about 10. Compared with the secondary growth of SSZ-13 membrane, in-situ synthesis of SSZ-13 membrane did not yield selective SSZ-13 membrane under current synthesis conditions. The pin-pore defects could be observed on the membrane surface for the typical membrane prepared for 48 h, as shown in Figure S2. It suggested that the pre-coated seeds supplied effective nuclei for the formation of an intergrowth SSZ-13 membrane.

texts.orig: To study the effect of SDA on the growth of SSZ-13 membrane, the membranes were also prepared by single TMAOH, single TEAOH, single TMAdaOH, the binary SDAs of TMAdaOH and TMAOH and the ternary combinations. Single TMAOH, single TEAOH and the binary SDAs of TMAdaOH and TMAOH yielded the discontinuous SSZ-13 layers and the bare supports can be observed in their SEM observations (not shown). The crystals were fully covered on the support surface when the single TMAdaOH and the ternary SDAs were used, as shown in Fig. 3. The concentration of TEAOH in the TMAdaOH -TEAOH system also affected the membrane quality, as shown in Table S2. The CO2/CH4 selectivity increased from 85 to 300 with the increase of TEAOH/SiO2 ratio of membrane gel from 0.25 to 0.5, but decreased to only 60 when TEAOH/SiO2 ratio increased to 1. The formation of higher defect density at higher TEAOH concentration could derive from that the increased alkalinity of gel had the increased dissolution to reduce the intergrowth of polycrystalline zeolite layers. These results indicate that the morphologies of supported zeolite layers are affected by the type and the concentration of SDA.

texts.orig: 3.2. The role of TEAOH
texts.text: 3.2. The role of TEAOH

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To better understand the role of the SDA of TEAOH on the SSZ-13 crystallization, SSZ-13 powders were prepared with single TMAdaOH and the binary SDAs of TMAdaOH and TEAOH. Synthesis conditions for the powder synthesis were similar to membrane synthesis except for synthesis time (synthesis time increases to 72 h). The sample prepared using the binary SDAs displayed the higher crystallinity based on their XRD patterns (Fig. 4). This result is consistent with previous reports [24,25], where the combinations of SDAs improved crystallization of SAPO-34 [24] and MWW zeolites [25] compared with a single SDA. Also, the crystals synthesized by the mixed SDAs had nearcubic morphology, and the crystals obtained by the single SDA displayed dodecahedron morphology, from their SEM observations in Fig. 5.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The 13 C MAS NMR spectra of SSZ-13 synthesized with single TMAdaOH and the binary SDAs of TMAdaOH and TEAOH are shown in Fig. 6. These spectra suggest two relevant facts: (1) TMAda þ is always found intact inside the SSZ-13 samples; (2) TEA þ is also found when used in the reaction mixture, although because of the overlapping of the expected resonances of TEA þ with those of TMAda þ . The SSZ-13 structure consists of CHA-cages and 8MR channels in the CHA topology. Because the sizes of TMAda þ (cross section of 0.66 -0.7 nm) and TEA þ (cross section of  0.7 nm) are larger than the 8 MR channel of SSZ-13, both cations are likely occluded in the CHA cage (0.73 nm) of SSZ13. TEA þ was also assigned to be present in the LTA cage (1.1 nm) other than in the 8MR channel (0.42 nm) of UZM-5 structure (LTA topology) due to the size exclusion [26]. Camblor et al. [25] reported that the crystallization rate of ITQ-1 was greatly improved when the combinative SDAs of TMAdaOH and HMI were used, compared with that prepared by TMAdaOH alone. The presence of both organics in ITQ-1 was con fi rmed by 13 C MAS NMR spectra. The increased crystallization rate was explained to be that the big TMAda þ (to stabilize the 12 MR cage) and the small HMI (to stabilize the 10 MR channel) took a ' cooperative structure-directing effect ' on the crystallization of ITQ. We regard that this explanation is suitable to our system: the role of TEAOH appears to be an additional stabilization effect by void-space fi lling of the CHA cage.

texts.text: 3.3. Single-gas permeances

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Single-gas permeances were measured for membrane M2 at 303 K and a feed pressure of 0.2 MPa using the Wicke -Kallenbach method. As shown in Fig. 7, the permeance decreased as their molecular size increased, expect for CO2. Even though the kinetic diameter of CO2 is larger than that of H2, the CO2 permeance was higher because SSZ-13 zeolite preferentially adsorbs CO2. The membrane was almost impermeable to C3H8 (lower than the chromatographic detection limit), which indicates that the membrane has few defects that are larger than 0.43 nm. The ideal CO2/CH4, C2H4/C2H6 and C2H4/C3H8 selectivities of membrane M2 were 360, 10 and 4 1000, respectively.

texts.orig: 3.4. Mixed-gas separation
texts.text: 3.4. Mixed-gas separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 1 shows the mixture permeation results of SSZ-13 membranes synthesized using different SDAs for equimolecular CO2/CH4 and C2H4/C2H6 mixtures at 303 K and a feed pressure of 0.2 MPa using the Wicke -Kallenbach technique. The best membrane (M2) had a CO2/CH4 selectivity of 300 and a CO2 permeance of 2.0 -10 � 7 mol/ (m 2 s Pa). This membrane also showed a C2H4/C2H6 selectivity of 11 and a C2H4 permeance of 2.9 -10 � 9 mol/(m 2 s Pa). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of C2H4 separation from C2H6 using zeolite membranes. The C2H4/C2H6 selectivity is higher than that of most polymeric membranes [14] and MOF membranes [11,13]. The small channel (pore) size of SSZ-13 (0.37 nm -0.42 nm) [17] favored the diffusion of CO2 (kinetic diameter E 0.33 nm) [11] over CH4 (kinetic diameter E 0.38 nm) [11], and C2H4 (kinetic diameter E 0.37 nm) [2] over C2H6 (kinetic diameter E 0.41 nm) [2]. The main separation mechanism for the two binary mixtures was attributed to the difference in molecular diffusivities which was resulted from the size differences. Adsorption isotherms on SSZ-13 crystals collected from the autoclave when the membrane was synthesized using TMAdaOH and TEAOH showed that the CO2/CH4 adsorption selectivity was 4.6. Thus, CO2 preferentially adsorption increases the CO2/CH4 separation selectivity of membrane. However, the sorption amount of C2H4 has been reported to be  17% lower than that for C2H6 on Si-CHA zeolite at 303 K [27]. The obtained C2H4/C2H6 selectivity could be the completing results between a preferential C2H4 diffusion selectivity and a preferential C2H6 adsorption [13].

texts.orig: The CO2/CH4 selectivities and permeances through membrane M2 as a function of feed pressure with sweep gas are shown in Fig. 8. The single-gas permeation is included in Fig. 8. Carbon dioxide permeance decreased as pressure drop increased, and CH4 permeance was almost independent of pressure drop. The CO2/ CH4 selectivity exhibited a maximum of 300 at pressure drop of 0.2 MPa and dropped off to 230 at 1 MPa. The CH4 single-gas and mixture permeances were almost the same, whereas the CO2 mixture permeances were 70 -80% of the single gas permeances at the range of feed pressure. Therefore the mixture selectivity was lower than the ideal selectivity.

texts.orig: The comparison of CO2/CH4 separation performance through zeolite membranes are listed in Table 3. Our one-layer SSZ-13 membrane prepared using symmetric supports had both higher CO2 permeance and higher selectivity than the previous two-layer SSZ-13 membranes [23]. This membrane also showed a higher CO2 permeance than the reported zeolite DDR [4] and zeolite T [3] membranes. The asymmetric alumina supported SAPO-34 membranes [30] displayed the highest CO2 permeance together with good selectivity among those membranes. The symmetric mullite support we used in this study is much cheaper than those for SSZ13 [23] and SAPO-34 [6,30] membranes preparation.

texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SSZ-13 membranes with extremely low defect concentrations were synthesized using dual structure-directing agents of TMAdaOH and TEAOH on symmetric tubular mullite supports in one hydrothermal synthesis step. The crystallization rate and morphologies SSZ-13 powders and membranes were in fl uenced by the SDA precursor. The separation selectivities of the best membrane for equimolar CO2/ CH4 and C2H4/C2H6 mixtures at room temperature were 300 and 11, respectively. Carbon dioxide permeance decreased more quickly than the permeances of C2H4, CH4 and C2H6 as feed pressure
